resume:
Daniel Okoye — Senior Customer Support Manager

Professional Summary
I am a senior customer support manager with more than nine years of experience leading frontline and escalation teams for consumer fintech and marketplace products. Over my career I have built support functions from the ground up, scaled teams through periods of hypergrowth, and owned the operational metrics that leadership cares about most: customer satisfaction, first-contact resolution, average handle time, and backlog health. I describe myself as a hands-on operator who is equally comfortable coaching an agent through a difficult call and presenting weekly operational reviews to a VP. I care deeply about doing right by customers while protecting the business, and I have spent a lot of time thinking about where those two goals come into tension, particularly around fraud, chargebacks, and account access.

Professional Experience

BrightPay (Consumer Fintech) — Senior Customer Support Manager, 2021 to present
In my current role I lead a team that handles inbound support for a consumer payments application used by millions of customers. My team is responsible for the full range of customer issues, from simple how-to questions to sensitive account-access and money-movement disputes. When I joined, the support organization was struggling with a large and growing backlog, inconsistent quality, and a customer satisfaction score that had been sliding for several quarters. I was brought in specifically to stabilize the operation and then to scale it responsibly. In my first few months I spent most of my time simply listening to calls and reading tickets so that I could understand where customers were getting stuck and where agents lacked the tools or the authority to help them quickly. From that diagnosis I rebuilt the escalation process so that there was a single clear path for sensitive cases, introduced a quality-assurance program with calibrated scoring that the whole team trusted because we calibrated scores together every week, and partnered closely with the risk and compliance teams to make sure our agents handle fraud and identity-verification cases correctly and consistently. I also own the relationship with our outsourced support vendor, including their staffing plan, their quality scores, and the way we route overflow volume to them during peak periods. I run the weekly business review with my director, where I present queue health, satisfaction trends, the largest drivers of contact volume, and any incidents that touched customer trust. A meaningful part of my job is making judgment calls on edge cases where the policy is ambiguous, especially when a frustrated customer is locked out of their own money and is escalating loudly. I have learned that those moments are exactly where a support organization either builds or destroys its credibility with the risk and compliance teams, because the easy thing and the correct thing are often not the same thing, and leadership is watching how you behave under that pressure.

MarketWave (Online Marketplace) — Customer Support Team Lead, 2017 to 2021
Before BrightPay I led a frontline support pod at an online marketplace connecting independent sellers with buyers. I started as a senior agent and was promoted to team lead within my first year. In that role I managed a team of around a dozen agents covering buyer and seller disputes, refunds, and policy enforcement. I built the first version of our refund-decision guidelines, trained new hires, and acted as the final escalation point for disputes that the team could not resolve on their own. This was where I first learned how much trust customers place in a support agent during a stressful moment, and how easily that trust can be damaged by an inconsistent or slow response. I also got my first real exposure to the tension between customer empathy and policy discipline, because every refund I approved outside of policy was money the business did not get back.

HelpDesk Plus (Outsourced Support Provider) — Support Agent, 2015 to 2017
My career in support began at an outsourced contact center where I handled tickets and calls across several different client accounts. This early experience taught me the fundamentals: how to de-escalate an angry customer, how to document a case clearly, and how to work within strict service-level targets. It was demanding work, but it gave me a deep respect for frontline agents that I carry with me as a manager today.

Skills and Competencies
Team leadership and coaching, escalation management, quality assurance and call calibration, workforce planning basics, vendor and BPO management, fraud and chargeback handling, identity verification workflows, customer communication during incidents, stakeholder reporting, and cross-functional partnership with risk and compliance.

Education
Bachelor of Arts in Business Administration.

What I am looking for
I am looking for a senior support leadership role where I can own a meaningful operation, continue to develop a strong team, and work on the hard problems at the intersection of customer experience, trust, and safety. I am especially drawn to environments where doing the right thing for the customer and protecting the business are treated as two sides of the same coin rather than as opposing forces.
jd: We are hiring a Senior Customer Support Manager for a consumer fintech product to own frontline and escalation support for account-access, money-movement, and fraud-related issues. You will be accountable for CSAT, first-contact resolution, backlog health, and — critically — for ensuring agents follow identity-verification and compliance procedures without exception, even under customer pressure. The role requires someone who can describe a clear, consistent timeline of how they handled a sensitive incident, who can articulate where customer empathy must yield to compliance, and who can speak precisely about the controls that protect both the customer and the business. You will partner daily with risk and compliance and present incident reviews to leadership.

history.q: Give me the shape of your current role — team size, the queues you own, and the metrics you're judged on.
When you joined BrightPay, walk me through the first 90 days. What did you do, and in what order?
Tell me about a sensitive account-access case where a customer was locked out of their own money.
Who verified the customer's identity in that case, and what step came before you released the account?
Take me through that incident strictly in order — what happened first, second, third?
I'm hearing the sequence shift. Be precise: did you reset the account before or after compliance signed off on the identity check?

candidate_last_answer: Okay, so — let me get this straight in my head. The customer called in really upset, locked out, and... right, so first I had the agent escalate it to me. Then I reset the account access so the customer could get back to their money, because they were threatening to go to social media and we couldn't afford that. And then we looped in compliance to do the identity verification after the fact. Wait — no, actually I think compliance was already involved earlier... Honestly the verification and the reset kind of happened in parallel that day. The point is the customer got taken care of. I mean, look, sometimes you have to make a judgment call and unlock first to calm the situation down, and we'd circle back on the formal ID check once the customer wasn't melting down on us. It worked out — the account turned out to be legitimate.

safety_flags: admitted resetting account access / releasing customer funds before completing the required identity verification, to avoid social-media escalation
top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: unlock first to calm the situation down, and we'd circle back on the formal ID check

expected-yield: force the candidate to commit to a single, ordered timeline and confront whether account access was actually released before identity verification completed — exposing whether this was a one-off lapse or a standing practice, and what the documented control actually required
